pi_items.source: Calendar verification: November 15, 2024 is a valid date (Friday). Found via https://www.almanac.com/calendar/date/2024-11-15
Aphids (Hemiptera: Aphididae) are real crop pests that are commonly documented in pest management. Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Soybean_aphid
Soybean is the primary host plant for Aphis glycines (soybean aphid). Source: https://ipmworld.umn.edu/ragsdale-soybean-aphid
Generic field identifier, contextually valid in farm management systems. Generic naming convention used in agricultural operations.

According to IPM research, soybean aphid economic threshold is 250 aphids/plant with >80% plant infestation (Source: https://www.mda.state.mn.us/sites/default/files/docs/2022-06/ipmsoybeanaphid_0.pdf). However, 30% could refer to plant infestation rate (below threshold) or defoliation severity. The value is plausible but unconventional.
Aphis glycines is the correct scientific name for the soybean aphid (Hemiptera: Aphididae). Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Soybean_aphid
Same date as observation_date. November 15, 2024 is valid. Same-day treatment after observation is consistent with IPM rapid response protocols.
Neem oil is a recognized organic pesticide effective against aphids including soybean aphids. Source: https://ipm.ucanr.edu/PMG/PESTNOTES/pn7404.html

For active aphid infestations, 2% neem oil solution (or 1.5-2%) is within recommended range. For dilution: 2% pure neem oil = ~1/3 cup per gallon of water. Source: https://growsgarden.com/neem-dilution/

Standard neem oil agricultural applications: maximum 0.5 gallons (~1.9 liters) neem oil per acre, diluted in minimum 25 gallons water (~95 liters total per acre). A backpack sprayer holds 3-5 gallons. If '4 liters' refers to total spray solution per acre, it would be insufficient (minimum 95L required). If it refers to concentrate, it would exceed typical maximum (1.9L). Interpretation unclear. Source: https://www3.epa.gov/pesticides/chem_search/ppls/070051-00002-20210416.pdf
Backpack sprayers (3-5 gallon capacity) are standard agricultural equipment for pesticide application and compatible with neem oil. Source: https://content.ces.ncsu.edu/calibrating-a-backpack-sprayer

12 liters equals approximately 3.2 gallons. This is plausible as quantity of diluted spray solution (backpack sprayers hold 3-5 gallons, so ~2-3 fill-ups) but metadata doesn't clarify if this is concentrate or diluted solution. Without more context, cannot fully verify.

Liters are a standard unit for measuring liquid volume in agricultural applications. Compatible with farmOS quantity measurement system (supports any unit of measure). Source: https://v1.farmos.org/guide/quantity/

privileged_info_summary: Task document_pest_management tests creation of two farmOS Field Kit logs: (1) Observation log documenting soybean aphid (Aphis glycines, scientific name verified) infestation on November 15, 2024; (2) Input log documenting neem oil treatment (2% concentration verified as appropriate for active infestations) applied with backpack sprayer on same date. Log type structure (Observation and Input) verified as real farmOS Field Kit log types. The 30% infestation level and '4 liters per acre' application rate could not be verified against standard IPM thresholds, though individual component data (neem oil effectiveness, 2% concentration, backpack sprayer use, date validity) are real and factual.
